hero.title:
What is Node.js,
really?
hero.subtitle: (and friends.)
hero.lead: A scribbled-in field guide to Node.js — the runtime, the event loop, npm, and why JavaScript escaped the browser in the first place.
hero.note: ← drag the thumb tabs on the right →

welcomeStickies.html:
✎ Before you start:

you don't need to know browser JS. you need to know how to read a callback without screaming.

✎ A promise:

no "build a Twitter clone in 4 hours." we'll do the boring parts first — they're the ones you'll actually need.

levels.label: BASIC
levels.intro: — the parts that make Node Node.

lessons.title: Node is just JavaScript with superpowers

sticky.html: Imagine your browser's JavaScript engine quit its day job, moved out of the tab, and got hired as a server. Same brain. Brand new toolbelt — files, sockets, processes.

blocks.html:
Node.js is V8 (Chrome's JS engine) plus a runtime that lets it touch your machine. The browser sandboxes JS away from the filesystem and the network. Node takes those walls down on purpose.

If your only mental model of JavaScript was "the thing that makes buttons wiggle on a webpage," prepare for a small existential moment.
blocks.placeholder: metaphor: V8 engine in a small green box labeled 'node', wires running to files, sockets, OS

blocks.label: WHAT NODE BUNDLES TOGETHER
blocks.caption: = Node.js. The same JS you already know, plus the things browsers refuse to give it.

bad.label: COMMON MISCONCEPTION
bad.html: "Node is a different language, like Python or Go."

good.label: MORE ACCURATE
good.html: "Node is the same JavaScript, run outside the browser, with a different standard library bolted on."

blocks.title: Under the hood — V8 and libuv
blocks.html:
V8 turns JavaScript source into fast machine code on the fly. libuv is the C library that gives Node its event loop and async I/O — it's what lets one process serve thousands of slow network connections without one thread per request.

Together: you write JavaScript that looks single-threaded, but the actual work (reading a file, talking to a socket) is happening in the background on a thread pool. The event loop is the receptionist that hands the results back to your code.

blocks.prompt: Before reading this — what did you think Node was for? Did your answer change?

lessons.title: The event loop is a very polite waiter

sticky.html: One waiter, full restaurant. They never stand still. They take an order, fire it to the kitchen, take the next order, drop off the appetizers from table 3, take another order. Nothing waits. Nobody is forgotten.

blocks.html:
That's the event loop. A single thread that hands slow work to the OS and keeps moving. It's why a Node server can hold thousands of connections at once.

The catch: if you do the slow work — a CPU-heavy loop, a giant JSON.parse — the waiter freezes. Every other table waits while you finish your math.
blocks.placeholder: metaphor: one waiter zigzagging between tables, plates flying, kitchen door swinging

items.question: Why does while(true){} freeze a Node server?
items.answer: Because the event loop is one thread. A tight synchronous loop never yields control, so no other callback — incoming request, timer, I/O completion — ever runs.

items.question: What's the difference between setImmediate and setTimeout(fn, 0)?
items.answer: setImmediate runs after the current poll phase. setTimeout(fn, 0) goes into the timers phase. In practice, in real code their order depends on context — but inside an I/O callback, setImmediate always wins.

blocks.title: Under the hood — phases of the loop
blocks.html:
The event loop runs in phases: timers, pending callbacks, idle/prepare, poll, check, close. Each phase has its own queue. Microtasks (promises, queueMicrotask) run between phases — which is why Promise.resolve().then(…) usually fires before a setImmediate queued in the same tick.

If you only remember one thing: don't block the loop. Hand long work to a worker thread, a child process, or split it into chunks.

blocks.prompt: Have you ever written code that 'froze' Node? What was the slow thing?

levels.label: INTERMEDIATE
levels.intro: — the things you'll spend most of your day with.

lessons.title: npm is a shared cupboard

sticky.html: Imagine a giant kitchen where every cook in the world dropped a jar of something they made. Some jars are gold. Some are mystery sauce from 2014. Most are fine.

blocks.html:
That's npm. A registry of JavaScript packages — millions of them. You name a thing, someone wrote a package for it. Probably three packages. The trick isn't installing — it's choosing.

The number-one Node-developer skill, in honesty: reading a package's last-commit date and weekly download count before you npm install anything.
blocks.placeholder: metaphor: huge wall of jars and packages, some glowing, some dusty, all labeled in JSON

blocks.label: ANATOMY OF A PACKAGE.JSON
blocks.html:
name →"my-thing"
scripts →"start", "test", "build" — the tasks you can run with npm run
dependencies →the jars you actually use at runtime
devDeps →the jars you only need while building or testing

bad.label: RISKY
bad.html:
npm i some-random-package-with-30-downloads

No source. No tests. One maintainer with a vague username.

good.label: SAFER
good.html:
npm i a-well-known-package

Check: weekly downloads, last commit, open issues, source on GitHub.

blocks.title: Under the hood — node_modules, lockfiles, and semver
blocks.html:
node_modules is where installed packages live. It can get enormous because every dep brings its own deps. package-lock.json pins exact versions so two developers (or your laptop and CI) get the same tree.

Versions follow semver: MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH. A ^1.2.3 in package.json means "any 1.x ≥ 1.2.3". A ~1.2.3 means "any 1.2.x ≥ 1.2.3". A bare 1.2.3 means exactly that.

blocks.prompt: What's the worst dependency you've inherited? What made it bad — abandoned, oversized, or just wrong fit?

outro.title: That's the Node field guide.
outro.subtitle: Now go ship something with it.

stickies.html:
✎ next volume idea:
"streams, buffers, and the parts of Node nobody teaches first."

✎ remember:
most production Node bugs are just the loop being blocked in a way you didn't notice.
